foreshadow_text:
Old Behrman was a painter who lived on the ground floor beneath them. He was past sixty and had a Michael Angelo’s Moses beard curling down from the head of a satyr along the body of an imp. Behrman was a failure in art. Forty years he had wielded the brush without getting near enough to touch the hem of his Mistress’s robe. He had been always about to paint a masterpiece, but had never yet begun it. For several years he had painted nothing except now and then a daub in the line of commerce or advertising. He earned a little by serving as a model to those young artists in the colony who could not pay the price of a professional. He drank gin to excess, and still talked of his coming masterpiece. For the rest he was a fierce little old man, who scoffed terribly at softness in any one, and who regarded himself as especial mastiff-in-waiting to protect the two young artists in the studio above.

Sue found Behrman smelling strongly of juniper berries in his dimly lighted den below. In one corner was a blank canvas on an easel that had been waiting there for twenty-five years to receive the first line of the masterpiece. She told him of Johnsy’s fancy, and how she feared she would, indeed, light and fragile as a leaf herself, float away when her slight hold upon the world grew weaker.
payoff_text:
And that afternoon Sue came to the bed where Johnsy lay, contentedly knitting a very blue and very useless woolen shoulder scarf, and put one arm around her, pillows and all.

“I have something to tell you, white mouse,” she said. “Mr. Behrman died of pneumonia to-day in the hospital. He was ill only two days. The janitor found him on the morning of the first day in his room downstairs helpless with pain. His shoes and clothing were wet through and icy cold. They couldn’t imagine where he had been on such a dreadful night. And then they found a lantern, still lighted, and a ladder that had been dragged from its place, and some scattered brushes, and a palette with green and yellow colors mixed on it, and—look out the window, dear, at the last ivy leaf on the wall. Didn’t you wonder why it never fluttered or moved when the wind blew? Ah, darling, it’s Behrman’s masterpiece—he painted it there the night that the last leaf fell.”
foreshadow_summary: Behrman, a failed artist, has always talked about painting a masterpiece but never started; a blank canvas has been waiting for 25 years.
payoff_summary: After Behrman dies, Sue reveals that he painted the last leaf on the wall during the storm, calling it his masterpiece.

proposed_trigger.description: On the stormy night when the last real leaf falls, Behrman goes out in the rain to paint a replacement leaf, leading to his pneumonia and death; the trigger is observable the next morning when his wet clothes and painting tools are found.
observable_conditions: P0036: 'A persistent, cold rain was falling, mingled with snow.'

P0055: 'The janitor found him... His shoes and clothing were wet through and icy cold. ... they found a lantern, still lighted, and a ladder ... and some scattered brushes, and a palette with green and yellow colors mixed on it'

relation_description: The blank canvas and Behrman's lifelong dream of a masterpiece are fulfilled by his selfless act of painting the leaf to save Johnsy's life.
stage1_rationale: The blank canvas and repeated mention of an unstarted masterpiece are a classic object/ promise setup, explicitly paid off when the painted leaf is called his masterpiece.

foreshadow_text:
“Six,” said Johnsy, in almost a whisper. “They’re falling faster now. Three days ago there were almost a hundred. It made my head ache to count them. But now it’s easy. There goes another one. There are only five left now.”

“Five what, dear. Tell your Sudie.”

“Leaves. On the ivy vine. When the last one falls I must go, too. I’ve known that for three days. Didn’t the doctor tell you?”
payoff_text:
And that afternoon Sue came to the bed where Johnsy lay, contentedly knitting a very blue and very useless woolen shoulder scarf, and put one arm around her, pillows and all.

“I have something to tell you, white mouse,” she said. “Mr. Behrman died of pneumonia to-day in the hospital. He was ill only two days. The janitor found him on the morning of the first day in his room downstairs helpless with pain. His shoes and clothing were wet through and icy cold. They couldn’t imagine where he had been on such a dreadful night. And then they found a lantern, still lighted, and a ladder that had been dragged from its place, and some scattered brushes, and a palette with green and yellow colors mixed on it, and—look out the window, dear, at the last ivy leaf on the wall. Didn’t you wonder why it never fluttered or moved when the wind blew? Ah, darling, it’s Behrman’s masterpiece—he painted it there the night that the last leaf fell.”
foreshadow_summary: Johnsy becomes fixated on the ivy leaves, counting them down and asserting that when the last one falls, she will die.
payoff_summary: After Johnsy recovers, Sue reveals that the last leaf was actually a painting by Behrman, which stayed on the wall despite the wind, preventing Johnsy from giving up.

proposed_trigger.description: The stormy night when the last real leaf falls, and Behrman, knowing Johnsy's belief, paints a leaf to make her think it's still there; observable trigger: the next morning, the leaf is still there after the storm, and later, the discovery of the lantern and paint.
observable_conditions: P0036: They 'peered out the window fearfully at the ivy vine' and then 'looked at each other for a moment without speaking' as the storm began.
P0040: After the storm, the leaf is still there, 'hung bravely'.
P0055: The truth about the painted leaf is revealed.

relation_description: Johnsy's psychological dependence on the leaves creates a life-or-death situation; Behrman's intervention breaks the cycle, with the hidden truth revealed after his death.
stage1_rationale: Johnsy's superstition is the central psychological driver; the payoff reveals the hidden mechanism that subverted her belief, making it a strong delayed setup.

foreshadow_text: But, lo! after the beating rain and fierce gusts of wind that had endured through the livelong night, there yet stood out against the brick wall one ivy leaf. It was the last on the vine. Still dark green near its stem, but with its serrated edges tinted with the yellow of dissolution and decay, it hung bravely from a branch some twenty feet above the ground.
payoff_text: “I have something to tell you, white mouse,” she said. “Mr. Behrman died of pneumonia to-day in the hospital. He was ill only two days. The janitor found him on the morning of the first day in his room downstairs helpless with pain. His shoes and clothing were wet through and icy cold. They couldn’t imagine where he had been on such a dreadful night. And then they found a lantern, still lighted, and a ladder that had been dragged from its place, and some scattered brushes, and a palette with green and yellow colors mixed on it, and—look out the window, dear, at the last ivy leaf on the wall. Didn’t you wonder why it never fluttered or moved when the wind blew? Ah, darling, it’s Behrman’s masterpiece—he painted it there the night that the last leaf fell.”
foreshadow_summary: The last ivy leaf is described with unusual detail, appearing both fresh and decayed, and remains impervious to the storm.
payoff_summary: Sue reveals that the leaf was painted by Behrman, explaining its strange appearance and persistence.

proposed_trigger.description: As the last real leaf falls during the storm, Behrman paints a fake one. The leaf's failure to move or fall in the wind is noticed when Johnsy demands the shade be raised.
observable_conditions: P0040: 'Still dark green near its stem, but with its serrated edges tinted with the yellow of dissolution and decay, it hung bravely...'
P0046: The leaf is still there after another night.
P0055: 'Didn't you wonder why it never fluttered or moved when the wind blew?'

relation_description: The seemingly realistic leaf description contains clues (its color and static nature) that retroactively signal it is a painting, a clever misdirection for the reader.
stage1_rationale: The description is subtle and only clearly signals the deception in retrospect; it may be missed on first reading, but it qualifies as a planted clue.
